title: COLLIE: Systematic Construction of Constrained Text Generation Tasks
authors: Shunyu Yao, Howard Chen, Austin W. Hanjie, Runzhe Yang, Karthik Narasimhan (Princeton University)
venue: Cited as ICLR 2024 per task's expected citation; arXiv abstract page itself lists no journal-ref/ICLR acceptance note (v1 only, submitted 17 Jul 2023). Venue attribution not independently confirmable from arXiv metadata alone but is not contradicted (per extraction header notes).
claim: COLLIE is a grammar-based framework that lets researchers specify rich, compositional text-generation constraints (over word/sentence/paragraph/passage levels, combining count and position operators with logical AND/OR) and then automatically extracts natural, corpus-grounded target values, renders them into natural-language instructions, and evaluates model generations against them. Using this framework the authors build COLLIE-v1 (2,080 instances / 13 constraint structures) and show that even GPT-4, the best of five evaluated instruction-tuned LLMs, only satisfies constraints 50.9% of the time on average in zero-shot generation, with difficulty strongly modulated by position (e.g., 100% success starting a sentence with a given word vs. 40-60% ending one with a given word) and by logical composition (stacking an extra base-constraint reliably hurts performance).
novelty: The authors present COLLIE's novelty as: (1) a formal context-free grammar (Eqs. 1-7) that can compositionally express arbitrary count/position constraints across four generation levels and combine them with logical AND/OR, which prior constrained-generation benchmarks (fixed single constraint type, e.g. include some words) cannot do; (2) an automatic pipeline that extracts constraint target values directly from real corpora (Wikipedia, CC-News, Gutenberg) rather than synthesizing wholly artificial targets, which they argue makes the benchmark more realistic than prior grammar-based synthetic benchmarks (e.g. BabyAI, CLEVR, COGS-style instruction-following grammars) that have 'limited linguistic diversity and practical applicability'; and (3) a rule-based renderer + parser/evaluator + natural-language feedback generator that make the whole framework 'extensible and lightweight' so new constraint types/data sources can be added with minimal effort, allowing the benchmark to 'co-evolve' with model capabilities (Sec 1, Sec 2 'Grammar-based compositional tests', Sec 6 Conclusion).

protocol.description: Primary experiments are single-shot/zero-shot: the model receives one rendered natural-language instruction per constraint instance and produces one generation, which is then parsed and checked against the constraint specification for a binary True/False per instance (Sec 4.1 step 4; Sec 5 intro: 'Our main experiments in this paper focus on a zero-shot prompting setup'). A one-shot variant was also tried as a preliminary/appendix check (Appendix C.1) using a fixed example input-output pair prepended per constraint structure, and found statistically similar to zero-shot (GPT-3.5: 23.1% vs 23.6%; GPT-4: 40.7% vs 39.4% -- Table 2), leading the authors to conclude the difficulty is about generation-under-constraint rather than instruction-understanding. CORRECTION: Appendix C.1 explicitly states this one-shot/zero-shot comparison was run on 'an internal version of the dataset before the current Collie-v1' -- i.e. these four numbers (23.1/23.6/40.7/39.4) are NOT measured on COLLIE-v1 itself and are not directly comparable to the 50.9% COLLIE-v1 zero-shot headline number reported elsewhere (Sec 1/Sec 5.1); the lower absolute values in Table 2 vs. the 50.9% headline corroborate that these come from a different (preliminary, pre-release) dataset, not a subset of COLLIE-v1. Separately, the paper also runs a genuinely interactive/multi-turn protocol (Sec 5.2 'Performance enhancement through feedback and interaction', Fig. 7): after each generation, COLLIE's rule-based (optionally GPT-polished) feedback renderer produces natural-language feedback stating which constraints were violated and how (Appendix A.2 gives a worked example), and the model is re-prompted for additional rounds (round 1 = zero-shot, rounds 2-4 = with feedback) -- this is the closest thing in the paper to an interaction loop with partial per-turn feedback rather than a single fresh forward pass.
protocol.observation_content: Per call, the model sees exactly one natural-language instruction rendered from a constraint specification via COLLIE's rule-based renderer, e.g. (Table 1) 'Generate a word with at least 15 letters.' or 'Generate a sentence with 10 words, where word 3 is "soft" and word 7 is "beach" and word 10 is "math".' The renderer (Appendix A.1) parses the constraint as a tree, generates a natural-language clause per base-constraint, and concatenates clauses for multi-constraints, starting from a template 'Please generate a {generation_level} with @...{target_level},'. No demonstrations/examples are shown in the main zero-shot condition. In the one-shot ablation (Appendix C.1), one fixed input-output example pair per constraint structure is prepended before the instruction. In the feedback/interactive condition (Appendix A.2, Sec 5.2), subsequent-round prompts additionally include feedback text such as: 'Your task is to generate a word with exactly 2 character 'v' and exactly 3 character 'i'. However, you generate a word with 3 character 'v' and 4 character 'i'.' (rule-based) or a GPT-polished paraphrase of the same. The model's own prior generation plus this feedback is what it sees going into the next round -- not a full transcript replay of all prior rounds (extraction does not specify whether full history is retained verbatim across rounds beyond this).

representations.summary: The paper does not compare alternative input/prompt *representations* (e.g., JSON vs. prose, different serializations) in the sense of a representation ablation; its only reported prompting-format comparison is zero-shot vs. one-shot (Appendix C.1, Table 2), which shows one-shot exemplars do not move accuracy (GPT-3.5: 23.1%->23.6%; GPT-4: 40.7%->39.4%). NOTE: per Appendix C.1, this comparison was run on 'an internal version of the dataset before the current Collie-v1', not on COLLIE-v1 itself, so these absolute numbers should not be treated as COLLIE-v1's zero-shot rate (which is 50.9% for GPT-4 per Sec 1/5.1). It also separately reports that the rule-based instruction can optionally be 'polished' by an LLM for fluency (Appendix A.1/A.2) but does not report an accuracy comparison between rule-based-only vs. GPT-polished instructions/feedback wording -- that comparison is not present in the extracted text.
representations.accuracy_effect: unverified — missing from extraction (no explicit rule-based vs. GPT-polished accuracy comparison found in the extracted text); the only quantified format effect is zero-shot vs. one-shot (Table 2, Appendix C.1), which showed no meaningful difference.

models.name: GPT-4 (gpt-4)
key_results: Average constraint satisfaction rate of only 50.9% despite leading all other models (Sec 1 Abstract/Intro).
100% success rate on constraining the first word of a sentence (start-of-sentence position constraint), vs. 40-60% success rate on ending a sentence with a specified word (Sec 1 Intro).
Average pass@20 rate above 63% across all constraints, vs. 32% for GPT-3.5 (Sec 5.1, Fig. 5).

Zero-shot 40.7% vs. one-shot 39.4% on a preliminary internal dataset predating COLLIE-v1, not COLLIE-v1 itself (Appendix C.1, Table 2: 'an internal version of the dataset before the current Collie-v1').
word03/para04/para05/pass01 only partially solved (40-70% satisfaction), and only by GPT-4 among the five models (Sec 5.1 'Constraints partially solved by GPT-4 only').

In the interactive-feedback setting, ~20% improvement after the 2nd round of feedback, but performance plateaus at 66% overall even after 3 additional feedback rounds -- comparable to pass@5 (Sec 5.2, Fig. 7). Task-level variation is large: word03's satisfaction rate is reported to change from 62.1% to 10% (as stated in the extracted text, Sec 5.2) while word02, sent01, sent02 remain challenging throughout.

Even where GPT-4 partially succeeds on last-character/position tasks, it frequently generates made-up/invalid words (e.g. 'coordinasor', 'adventudposis') rather than true satisfying answers (Appendix C.3, Figs. 11-12).

models.name: GPT-3.5-turbo
key_results: Average pass@20 rate of 32%, well below GPT-4's 63%+ (Sec 5.1, Fig. 5).
Zero-shot 23.1% vs. one-shot 23.6% on a preliminary internal dataset predating COLLIE-v1, not COLLIE-v1 itself (Appendix C.1, Table 2).
Ranked second overall behind GPT-4, ahead of PaLM-2 'with a large gap' (Sec 5.1).
On extreme letter-position tasks (e.g., i-th letter constrained to 'r'), GPT-3.5 manages to still generate valid words while GPT-4 fabricates non-words to satisfy the constraint (Appendix C.3).

models.name: PaLM-2 (text-bison-001)
key_results: Follows GPT-3.5 'with a large gap', then followed closely by the smaller open-source models (Sec 5.1).
2 of 1,132 constraint prompts were blocked by the PaLM-2 API guardrails during evaluation (Appendix C, examples given: ccnews_c07 and ccnews_c14).

models.name: Vicuna-7B
key_results: GPT-4 achieves 'more than twice the constraint satisfaction rate' of non-GPT models including Vicuna-7B (Sec 5.1).
Failed to deliver any satisfactory performance on tasks GPT-4 only partially solved (word03, para04, para05, pass01) (Sec 5.1).

models.name: Alpaca-7B
key_results: Performance trend places Alpaca-7B closely alongside Vicuna-7B at the bottom of the five-model ranking (Sec 5.1).
Failed to deliver any satisfactory performance on the GPT-4-only-partial tasks (Sec 5.1).

headline_numbers: GPT-4 average constraint satisfaction rate: 50.9% zero-shot across COLLIE-v1 (Sec 1 Abstract).
Position effect: 100% success starting a sentence with a specified word vs. 40-60% success ending a sentence with a specified word, for GPT-4 (Sec 1 Intro; Sec 5.2 'Position effect', Fig. 5).
Pass@20: GPT-4 >63% average vs. GPT-3.5 32% average (Sec 5.1, Fig. 5).

Zero-shot vs. one-shot: GPT-3.5 23.1% -> 23.6%; GPT-4 40.7% -> 39.4% (Appendix C.1, Table 2, measured on a preliminary internal dataset predating COLLIE-v1, not COLLIE-v1 itself) -- one-shot exemplars do not help.
Interactive feedback: GPT-4 gains ~20% after round 2, plateaus at 66% through rounds 3-4, comparable to pass@5 (Sec 5.2, Fig. 7).

Dataset scale: COLLIE-v1 = 2,080 constraint instances, 13 constraint structures, 1,435 unique constraint prompts, from 3 corpora (Wiki, CC-News, Gutenberg) (Sec 4 intro). [Note: Sec 6 Conclusion states '1,132 constraints from 13 different types' -- this discrepancy vs. the Sec 4 count of 2,080 appears in the source text itself and is preserved verbatim, not reconciled by the extraction.]
failure_modes: Logical composition increases difficulty even when the added constraint looks trivial in isolation: sent03 (word01 + an added sentence-level word-count constraint) is uniformly harder than word01 alone for every model (Sec 5.2 'Increased difficulty with logical composition').

Exact-equality count constraints are harder than range or one-sided-bound constraints: sent01 (exact character count) > para03 (range) > word01/sent03/para04 (bound only), in difficulty (Sec 5.2 'Counting level effect').
Counting characters within a word is easier than counting characters within a sentence (Sec 5.2 'Counting level effect').

Arbitrary (non-first, non-last) position constraints are uniformly hard for all models; only first-position constraints are reliably solved, and only GPT-4 shows partial success on last-position constraints (Sec 5.2 'Position effect').

Models (especially GPT-4) frequently satisfy position/length constraints by fabricating non-words (e.g., 'coordinasor', 'adventudposis') rather than producing valid English words, especially under tight letter-position constraints (Appendix C.3, Fig. 11).

Iterative feedback yields diminishing returns: after an initial ~20% jump at round 2, GPT-4 performance plateaus around 66% through rounds 3-4 and some constraint types (word02, sent01, sent02) remain essentially unsolved regardless of feedback (Sec 5.2, Fig. 7).

Small/open-source models (Vicuna-7B, Alpaca-7B) fail almost entirely on any task requiring more than simple existence-style constraints, even when GPT-4 only partially succeeds (Sec 5.1 'Constraints partially solved by GPT-4 only').
API-level failures: 2 of 1,132 prompts triggered PaLM-2 content-guardrail blocks unrelated to constraint difficulty (Appendix C).
one_shot_vs_multistep: In a single forward pass (zero-shot), COLLIE finds that even the best model (GPT-4) satisfies compositional constraints only about half the time on average (50.9%) on COLLIE-v1. Separately, on a preliminary internal dataset predating COLLIE-v1 (Appendix C.1: 'an internal version of the dataset before the current Collie-v1'), the authors find that providing a single input-output demonstration (one-shot) does not meaningfully change GPT-4/GPT-3.5 accuracy (Table 2: GPT-4 40.7% vs 39.4%, GPT-3.5 23.1% vs 23.6% -- note these numbers are lower than and not directly comparable to the 50.9% COLLIE-v1 headline figure since they come from a different dataset), which the authors interpret as evidence that the bottleneck is the generation-under-constraint capability itself, not instruction comprehension. Across an interaction loop with automatically generated natural-language feedback per turn (multi-round, up to 4 rounds) run on COLLIE-v1, GPT-4 does improve substantially in early rounds (~20% gain after round 2) but plateaus at 66% overall satisfaction after rounds 3-4 -- comparable to what plain repeated sampling (pass@5) already achieves -- so more turns/demonstrations help but do not close the gap to a high ceiling, and some constraint types (word02, sent01, sent02) stay unsolved regardless of how much feedback or how many samples are given (Sec 5.2, Fig. 7).

relation_to_candidate.position: COLLIE's main protocol is single-shot/zero-shot (one instruction -> one generation -> binary True/False per instance), matching c22's single-call structure; COLLIE additionally runs a genuinely interactive multi-round variant with per-turn natural-language feedback (which c22 does not use -- c22 is strictly single-call/all-pass). What is observed by the model each call is one rendered natural-language instruction only (no per-checker breakdown until/unless the feedback condition is invoked). Output shape in COLLIE ranges from a single word up to a full multi-paragraph passage (four generation levels: word/sentence/paragraph/passage), unlike c22 whose output is fixed to 'a few words'. The verifier in COLLIE is a deterministic parser per constraint specification (True/False per full instance, i.e. all base-constraints in the multi-constraint M must hold for satisfaction, matching c22's strict all-pass 0/1 semantics) -- but COLLIE's grammar itself has no logical negation-as-branch/Selection ('if P then A else B') operator; its grammar (Eq. 2) offers only conjunction (AND) and disjunction (OR) of base-constraints, not conditional branching on an input property.
relation_to_candidate.overlap: Both are grammar/library-defined, deterministically-checkable, compositional constrained-generation tasks scored 0/1 on whether all component constraints hold; both draw constraint atoms from a small typed vocabulary (count constraints, position constraints) and stack several atoms (COLLIE's multi-constraint structures like word02/word03/sent02/sent03/para02-05/pass01 already compose 2-4 base-constraints, directly paralleling c22's 3-5 composed checkers); both use natural corpora / natural-feeling instructions rather than pure synthetic nonsense (COLLIE by extracting real target values from Wikipedia/CC-News/Gutenberg; c22 by generating synthetic base micro-tasks plus real constraint atoms); both log fine-grained per-constraint pass/fail as diagnostic signal (COLLIE's feedback renderer states exactly which base-constraints failed and how, Appendix A.2; c22 logs per-checker verdicts as diagnostic facts).
relation_to_candidate.differences: COLLIE's constraint vocabulary is built from exactly two primitive operators -- count(.) and pos(.) -- composed only with AND/OR; it has no conditional/Selection operator ('if property P then rule A else rule B') that must be discovered rather than stated, which is the specific anti-one-shot-enumeration mechanism the c22 verdict identifies as carrying its entire non-guessability argument. COLLIE's instructions state every constraint explicitly in the prompt (nothing is hidden or must be inferred); c22's Selection constraints are deliberately withheld/implicit. COLLIE's checker library is grammar-general-purpose (position/count over four text levels) rather than IFEval/IFBench's broader instruction-type taxonomy (casing, forbidden words, keyword frequency, format wrappers, etc.) that c22 actually vendors as its oracle. COLLIE has no notion of a 'trivial base task' separate from the constraint -- the constraint specification directly determines what is generated (e.g. 'a word', 'a sentence') rather than gating an otherwise-independent micro-task (e.g., c22's 'Name a color.' plus stacked constraints). COLLIE's dataset is a fixed, released, frozen benchmark (COLLIE-v1, 2,080/1,435 instances) rather than an infinite synthetic generator with a seedable RNG, which is the opposite of what c22 needs (contamination-proof, regenerable pool) and is the gap the c22 dossier's own implementation-lens notes (net-new stacking generator, seed plumbing) already identify relative to IFBench, not COLLIE.
relation_to_candidate.what_c22_would_add: Relative to COLLIE specifically, c22 would add: (1) a conditional Selection/branching constraint type ('if P then A else B') absent from COLLIE's AND/OR-only grammar, which is exactly the mechanism c22's verdict leans on for anti-enumeration; (2) a decoupled trivial base task (independent of the constraint set) that the constraints gate, rather than COLLIE's constraint-defines-the-generation-target design; (3) an infinite, seed-driven synthetic generator (vs. COLLIE's frozen, corpus-extracted, released v1 dataset) for contamination resistance and reproducible difficulty scaling; (4) a broader/OOD checker taxonomy (IFBench-style casing, forbidden-letter, end-token, keyword atoms) beyond COLLIE's count/position-only primitives. COLLIE's own experiments would be directly useful as a baseline-design reference for c22 on: how much composition depth degrades accuracy (logical-composition finding), how position (first/last/arbitrary) modulates difficulty, how exact-vs-range-vs-bound count constraints differ in difficulty, and how little one-shot exemplars help vs. how much iterative feedback helps (with a plateau, not a solve).

corpus_claim: COLLIE (Yao et al., ICLR 2024) is the grammar-based synthetic constrained-generation generator; the COLLIE-style checker library/grammar defines c22's core generation approach.
corpus_claim_verdict: partially-supported. COLLIE is indeed a grammar-based framework for constrained text generation with an automatic extraction/rendering/evaluation pipeline, matching the 'grammar-based ... generator' half of the claim, and its count/position grammar is a plausible structural ancestor for a 'checker library'-style constraint approach. However, two aspects need correction: (1) COLLIE is not purely synthetic -- its constraint *target values* are deliberately extracted from real corpora (Wikipedia, CC-News, Project Gutenberg) specifically so that constraints admit natural, plausible answers (Sec 4.1); the paper explicitly contrasts itself with prior 'synthetic' grammar-based benchmarks (BabyAI/CLEVR-style) that it says have 'limited linguistic diversity' (Sec 2 'Grammar-based compositional tests'), so calling COLLIE itself 'synthetic' overstates/mischaracterizes it. (2) Per the c22 dossier's own implementation notes and repo review, c22's actual shipped/vendored checker oracle is the IFEval/IFBench checker library (allenai/IFBench), not COLLIE's code; COLLIE is cited as an academic-lineage/grammar precedent, not as the literal code being reused. The venue attribution 'ICLR 2024' also could not be independently verified from arXiv metadata per the extraction header (though not contradicted).
baseline_relevant: Instance/difficulty dials used: generation level (word/sentence/paragraph/passage: 4 levels), and constraint composition depth (5 of 13 constraint structures are single-level/single-constraint, 8 are multi-level/multi-constraint compositions of 2-4 base-constraints) (Sec 4.1).

Metric: binary constraint satisfaction (True/False per instance) averaged into a 'Success Rate'/'constraint satisfaction rate'; also pass@k (k up to 20) computed by sampling the same prompt repeatedly at temperature 0.7 (Sec 3 step 4; Sec 5 intro; Fig. 4).
Sampling settings: temperature 0.7 default; 20 trials/prompt for GPT-3.5/GPT-4, 30 for PaLM-2, 5-10 for Vicuna-7B/Alpaca-7B (Sec 5 intro, Appendix C.2).

Floors/ceilings observed: GPT-4 zero-shot average 50.9% (best model); GPT-4 pass@20 ceiling >63% but 'far from perfect' (Sec 5.1); iterative feedback plateau at 66% after 3+ rounds (Sec 5.2); word01/sent04/para01 near-ceiling for all models (existence-type constraints are easy); word02/sent01/sent02/para03 remain very challenging for every model including GPT-4 (arbitrary position + mixed counting levels) (Sec 5.1).

Prompt format effect: one-shot exemplars do not move accuracy relative to zero-shot (GPT-3.5 23.1%->23.6%, GPT-4 40.7%->39.4%; Appendix C.1, Table 2 -- measured on a preliminary internal dataset predating COLLIE-v1, not on COLLIE-v1 itself) -- suggests for baseline design that adding demonstrations is unlikely to meaningfully raise/lower an un-optimized baseline's score on this constraint-satisfaction style of task, and that the interesting variance comes from constraint composition/position, not from prompt exemplar format.

Contamination/data-release status: COLLIE-v1 (2,080 instances / 1,435 unique prompts per Sec 4, though Sec 6 states '1,132 constraints' -- discrepancy present in source text) is a fixed, publicly released dataset (code/data at https://collie-benchmark.github.io, code to be MIT-licensed per Appendix B.4) built from three corpora (Wikipedia CC BY-SA/GFDL, CC-News per Common Crawl ToS, Project Gutenberg public domain) with no PII per author claim (Appendix B.4) -- i.e., COLLIE-v1 itself is a frozen, potentially contamination-exposed benchmark (all experiments run July 2023, Sec 5 intro), unlike a live seedable generator; for an un-optimized c22-style baseline needing a fresh/regenerable pool, COLLIE's extraction *pipeline* (not its released v1 instances) would need to be re-run against new corpus samples to avoid staleness.

Known evaluation artifacts: 2/1,132 prompts blocked by PaLM-2 API content guardrails unrelated to constraint difficulty (Appendix C) -- a reminder to budget for API-refusal noise separate from true constraint failures when designing an automated 0/1 scorer.

Additional non-primary metrics available in the framework but not part of the core 0/1 score: word-validity check against an English word list (to catch fabricated non-words satisfying letter constraints, Appendix C.3) and GPT-4-as-judge coherence scoring (1-10 scale) for passage-level outputs (Appendix C.3) -- both illustrate the checker-only 0/1 score can be gamed by non-word outputs, relevant to c22's exact-word-count/casing/keyword checkers.
quotes: "We perform zero-shot evaluations of five state-of-the-art LLMs of varying sizes including GPT-4 [28] and PaLM [1]. While GPT-4 comparatively performs the best, it still achieves an average constraint satisfaction rate of only 50.9%." (Sec 1, Introduction)

"we find that challenges correlate with position -- for instance, instructing models to begin a sentence with a specific word leads to a 100% success rate for GPT-4, while asking models to end a sentence with a particular word results in a success rate of 40%-60%." (Sec 1, Introduction)
"Our main experiments in this paper focus on a zero-shot [footnote: We tried one-shot prompting and find GPT performances similar to zero-shot, see Section C.1.] prompting setup..." (Sec 5, opening)
"the average pass@20 rate of GPT-4 was above 63% across all constraints, significantly higher than the 32% achieved by GPT-3.5" (Sec 5.1, 'Constraints remaining very challenging')

"We observe a significant 20% improvement in GPT-4 performance after the second round of feedback. However, the model's performance plateaus at 66% even after three additional rounds of feedback, comparable to pass@5." (Sec 5.2, 'Performance enhancement through feedback and interaction')

"Task sent03 serves as an example of this, adding an extra constraint at the sentence level compared to task word01... performance on task sent03 uniformly trails behind that on task word01" (Sec 5.2, 'Increased difficulty with logical composition')
"M -> C | C ∧ M | C ∨ M (multi-constraint)" (Sec 3, Eq. 2 -- confirms grammar supports only AND/OR composition, no conditional/Selection branching operator)

"results are very similar for both GPT-3.5 and GPT-4, which suggests the task difficulty is mainly about generation under the constraint instead of understanding the constraint (by similar examples)." (Appendix C.1)

corrections: Fixed mischaracterization of the Appendix C.1 zero-shot vs. one-shot numbers (GPT-3.5 23.1%/23.6%, GPT-4 40.7%/39.4%, Table 2): the source explicitly states this comparison was run on 'an internal version of the dataset before the current Collie-v1,' not on COLLIE-v1 itself. The dossier previously presented these figures as if measured on COLLIE-v1 (e.g. describing it as 'the ablation subset' / 'across all constraints' of the benchmark), which would wrongly imply they are directly comparable to the 50.9% COLLIE-v1 zero-shot headline number. Added explicit correction notes to protocol.description, representations.summary, models[GPT-4].key_results, models[GPT-3.5-turbo].key_results, headline_numbers, one_shot_vs_multistep, and baseline_relevant.
